answer:
**In Manusmṛti chapter 2, the text establishes the Brahmin's superior authority—likened to a paternal role—over other varṇas (notably the Kṣatriya, represented by a king) primarily through knowledge of the Veda, which outweighs age, wealth, kinship, or action as the grounds for respect.**[^1][^2]

This hierarchy is explicit in verses 135–137, where even a young Brahmin commands respect over an elderly king, with knowledge ranked highest among the five grounds for deference across the upper varṇas.[^3] The chapter emphasizes Vedic study and initiation as defining the twice-born (dvija) status of Brahmins, Kṣatriyas, and Vaiśyas, but Brahmins' preeminence stems from their role as bearers of sacred knowledge, as reinforced throughout the student (brahmacārin) duties and etiquette rules.[^4]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, ch.2, [p-369b0d](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-369b0d). "A Brahmana of ten years and a king of a hundred years should be known as father and son; but of the two, the Brahmana is the father. // Mn_2.135 //"

[^2]: Manusmṛti, ch.2, [p-4648f6](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-4648f6). "Wealth, kinship, age, action, and knowledge as the fifth—these are the grounds for respect; each succeeding one is more weighty. // Mn_2.136 //"

[^3]: Manusmṛti, ch.2, [p-3ebb05](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-3ebb05). "Whichever man of the three upper *varnas* possesses the most of these five qualities in abundance is worthy of respect... // Mn_2.137 //"

[^4]: Manusmṛti, ch.2, [p-5ac6c9](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-5ac6c9). (Chapter close, tying perfection to Vedic studentship: "A Brahmana who thus practices his studentship without breaking his vow goes to the highest abode... // Mn_2.249 //"); cf. also [p-7223f8](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-7223f8) ("Not by years... he who is learned among us is the great one. // Mn_2.154 //").
